text: Robotic spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. A thirty-five-year-old female was found to have a distal pancreatic mass on a CT angio performed for a PE in the postpartum period. She was found to have a distal pancreatic mass on subsequent dedicated pancreatic CT scan consistent with a probable solid pseudopapillary tumor.

The DaVinci XI robot is utilized with a peri-umbilical camera port for visualization. A right lateral port accommodates a fenestrated tip-up grasper for retraction, while a left lateral abdominal port holds another fenestrated grasper. A left mid-abdominal port is used for robotic stapling and vessel sealing. Additionally, a five millimeter assistance port in the left lower quadrant aids in suction and exposure.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic omentum to expose the pancreas in the retroperitoneum, extending the dissection laterally to the lowest short gastric vessels and medially to reveal the pancreas proximal to the pancreatic mass.

The surgeon lifts the inferior border of the pancreas and peripancreatic tissues, carrying the dissection laterally towards the tail of the pancreas. This maneuver exposes the tumor on the inferior aspect of the tail of the pancreas posteriorly. The dissection in the periopancreatic plane elevates the tumor and the tail of the pancreas away from the retroperitoneum, revealing the splenic vein along the superior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon dissects along the superior border of the pancreas, separating the splenic vein from the pancreas both proximally and distally. Tributaries of the splenic vein to the tail of the pancreas are sealed using a vessel sealer. Distally, the pancreas is dissected away from the hilum of the spleen inferiorly.

The surgeon dissects the vascular supply to the periphery of the inferior spleen, exposing the distal inferior branch of the splenic vein. The dissection proceeds anteriorly and posteriorly to the branch, aiming to remove the distal pancreas along with the specimen.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the pancreatic tissue, ensuring complete removal of the distal pancreas while preserving the splenic venous and arterial branches.

The surgeon dissects both anteriorly and posteriorly to the distal splenic vessels, lifting the pancreatic tail with the specimen. The portion of the pancreatic tail extending behind the splenic vein is dissected and transected using a vessel sealer.

The splenic vein is visibly split into two branches, with the inferior branch positioned adjacent to the tail of the pancreas. The surgeon transects the tissue just distal to the tail of the pancreas, facilitating the complete mobilization of the pancreatic tail.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to detach small attachments from the inferior pole of the spleen, fully mobilizing the specimen. A blue loaded stapler is used to transect the specimen, leaving a cuff of normal pancreas proximal to the splenic tumor. The specimen is placed in an endo-catch bag and extracted through an expanded stapling port.
A XIX Blake drain is placed through a left lateral abdominal incision. The spleen appears well vascularized at the completion of the procedure.
